description: Confounder: The model predicts pd.NA == np.nan returns False (like NaT), but pd.NA propagate

_meta.explanation: The rule 'When you use `pd.isna()` to check for missing values, it returns True for all three NA types (float NaN, pd.NA, and pd.NaT), but using `== np.nan` only catches float NaN and silently returns False for pd.NA and pd.NaT.' does NOT hold here. The model predicts pd.NA == np.nan returns False (like NaT), but pd.NA propagates NA through comparisons, returning <NA> not False, so the output differs from the expected [False, False, False]
_meta.curriculum_note: Rule 'When you use `pd.isna()` to check for missing values, it returns True for all three NA types (float NaN, pd.NA, and pd.NaT), but using `== np.nan` only catches float NaN and silently returns False for pd.NA and pd.NaT.' holds in the obvious case but breaks here. Distractor B is the hard negative — the rule naively applied.
